does: This paper introduces DeNEVIL, a prompt generation algorithm that systematically elicits unethical responses from large language models to reveal their underlying moral vulnerabilities and value inclinations. The authors construct MoralPrompt, a dataset of 2,397 prompts spanning 500+ ethical principles grounded in Moral Foundation Theory, and use it to benchmark the intrinsic values of multiple LLMs. They find that most models exhibit significant misalignment with ethical values and propose VILMO, an in-context alignment method that substantially improves models' ethical value compliance without requiring additional training. The approach moves beyond conventional discriminative evaluations by using generative testing to expose hidden value vulnerabilities.
connects: This work builds on growing research into LLM alignment and safety, extending beyond narrow studies of specific biases toward comprehensive ethical value assessment. It draws on Moral Foundation Theory from moral psychology to structure ethical evaluation, moving away from ad-hoc fairness metrics toward theoretically grounded frameworks. The paper responds to limitations in existing discriminative evaluation methods that rely on multiple-choice questions with poor reliability, similar to criticisms raised in robustness and adversarial testing literature. It connects to prior work on value alignment in AI systems and complements research on constitutional AI and instruction tuning by systematically characterizing which ethical principles LLMs violate before attempting correction.
enables: This work enables more comprehensive ethical auditing of LLMs by providing both a systematic methodology for eliciting value violations and a reusable benchmark dataset. The MoralPrompt dataset and evaluation framework can support future development of alignment techniques and help researchers understand which ethical principles are most vulnerable across different model families. VILMO's in-context alignment approach suggests a practical direction for improving ethical compliance without expensive retraining. The findings motivate further research into why LLMs develop particular value misalignments and how different architectural choices, training procedures, or data compositions influence ethical value representation. This work establishes foundations for integrating moral philosophy more systematically into LLM development pipelines.
